coredata.dc:title: Au/graphene quantum dots/ferroferric oxide composites as catalysts for the solvent-free oxidation of alcohols
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaochen
Guo, Shouwu
Zhang, Jingyan
coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots and Au nanoparticles (GQDs/Au) are immobilized on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, forming GQDs/Au/Fe3O4 ternary composites. The as-prepared ternary composites exhibit superparamagnetic property rendering them easy to be isolated from the reaction mixture. More importantly, they show superb catalytic activity for solvent-free oxidation of VA and other alcohols that contain an aromatic benzyl group, to the corresponding aldehydes exclusively with air as oxidant. The great stability and selectivity of the GQDs/Au/Fe3O4 indicate that they might be applicable catalysts for the oxidation of aromatic alcohols.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites
Oxidation
Alcohols
Solvent-free reaction
Nanocomposites
Magnetic materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM and TEM images of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (a, b), modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles (c, d), and Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites (e, f).
XRD (a) and magnetic hysteresis loop (b) of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles; FT-IR (c) and Zeta potential (d) of the modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

Effect of temperature (a) and the amount of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 (b) without base on the oxidation of VA; (c) effect of temperature on the oxidation of VA with K2CO3; (d) reusability of the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4. Reaction conditions: (a) 50mg of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4, 1.2g of VA, 36h; (b) 1.2g of VA, 140°C; (c) 50mg of K2CO3, 50mg of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4, 1.2g of VA, 36h.
The oxidation of alcohols with Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites.

head.ce:title: Au/graphene quantum dots/ferroferric oxide composites as catalysts for the solvent-free oxidation of alcohols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of New Drug Design, School of Pharmacy, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of New Drug Design, School of Pharmacy, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots and Au nanoparticles (GQDs/Au) are immobilized on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, forming GQDs/Au/Fe3O4 ternary composites. The as-prepared ternary composites exhibit superparamagnetic property rendering them easy to be isolated from the reaction mixture. More importantly, they show superb catalytic activity for solvent-free oxidation of VA and other alcohols that contain an aromatic benzyl group, to the corresponding aldehydes exclusively with air as oxidant. The great stability and selectivity of the GQDs/Au/Fe3O4 indicate that they might be applicable catalysts for the oxidation of aromatic alcohols.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its convenience for separation and recovery through simple magnetic interaction, superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been widely used in catalysis [1]. Also, Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be used as supporting matrices for nanosized catalysts [1,2]. We previously found that the composite of Au nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (Au/GQDs) can catalyze the oxidation of veratryl alcohol (VA) to veratryl aldehyde or veratric acid with efficient conversion and excellent selectivity [3]. However, given the nanoscale size of the Au/GQDs, it is hard to recover them from the liquid reaction mixture except for using ultrahigh speed centrifugation. This limits somehow the practical applications of Au/GQDs as catalyst. To overcome the drawback of the hard separation and recovering of Au/GQDs from the VA oxidation reaction system, and to improve the recyclability, in this work, the Au/GQDs are immobilized on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, obtaining the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites with moderate magnetic property, which makes the recovery of the catalyst much convenient. In our previous work, the oxidation product was obtained through a complicated extraction procedure when VA oxidation was carried out in aqueous solution, which consumed large quantities of organic solvent and accumulates massive waste. Therefore, the catalytic activity of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites in the oxidation of VA and other alcohols with air as oxidant without solvent is explored in this work, since some alcohols are liquid.

Au/GQDs were prepared as described in our previous work [3,4]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared through a solvothermal procedure [5]. 300mg of Fe3O4 were dispersed in a mixture of ethanol (60mL), H2O (1mL) and NH3·H2O (1.5mL, 28wt%), 195μL of tetraethoxysilane and 780μL of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane were added quickly, mechanically stirred for 6h to get the modified Fe3O4. 1mg/mL of as-modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles was mixed with 0.15mg/mL 100mL of Au/GQDs under pH 4 and stirred for 1h to get the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4.

In a typical procedure, 1.2g of alcohol was added into an open round-bottomed flask and heated to the desired temperature (100°C) until dissolved. 50mg of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 and 50mg of K2CO3 were added under vigorous stirring, and kept under 100°C for 36h. The Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 were separated through magnetic absorption, the products were analyzed using GC–MS.

TEM (JEM-2010, Japan), FE-SEM (Zeiss ultra 55, Germany), XRD (D8-Advance, Bruker, Germany), vibrating sample magnetometer (Lakeshore 736, USA), FT-IR (VERTEX 700, BRUKER), Zeta potentials (Nicomp 380/ZLS, USA) and GC–MS analysis (Agilent 7890A gas chromatograph, 5975C mass spectrometer, USA) were used to characterized the samples.

The morphology of Au/GQDs is shown in Fig. S1 [3]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 1, the spherical Fe3O4 particles assuming average diameter of 390nm are aggregated with a number of smaller Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The formation of Fe3O4 is confirmed with XRD ( Fig. 2a) and matches well with the crystalline Fe3O4 (JCPDS 65-3107). The Fe3O4 nanoparticles assume typical superparamagnetic properties with 65.7emu/g saturation magnetization, which is enough for the separation and recovering of catalysts using external magnetic field. To immobilize Au/GQDs on Fe3O4, the surfaces of Fe3O4 nanoparticles are first modified with tetraethoxysilane and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane. Fig. 1c and d show images of silicon coated Fe3O4 nanoparticles, in which a thin film of ~10nm with uniform thickness forms on their surface. The FT-IR spectrum shows an obvious vibration at 1054–1143cm−1 corresponding to the Si–O bond, confirming the silica modification. The Zeta potential demonstrates that the Fe3O4 is negatively charged in a wide pH range before modification; while after the modification, the surface is positively charged when pH<10, proving the modified Fe3O4 are enriched with –NH2 groups. The surfaces of Au/GQDs are negatively charged, therefore, Au/GQDs should be immobilized onto the modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles through electrostatic interaction. As expected, Fig. 1e and f illustrates the Au/GQDs can be uniformly immobilized on the Fe3O4. The aggregation of the Au/GQDs particles is prevented successfully, which is preferable as a catalyst [6–8].

To evaluate the catalytic activity of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4, the solvent-free oxidation of VA is conducted utilizing air as oxidant. VA is chosen because it is a typical model compound for studying the lignin oxidation [3]. The oxidation of VA took place and only veratraldehyde was produced ( Fig. 3a). Notably, the conversion increases with the rising of the reaction temperature. The conversion of VA to veratraldehyde at 80°C was only ~2% in 36h, and reached to 21% at 140°C. The reaction would not take place without Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 (Fig. 3b). To further improve the conversion rate, a reaction of VA with increased amount of the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 was performed (Fig. 3b). Clearly, the reaction rate was accelerated dramatically, proving the catalytic ability of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 under solvent-free condition.

The catalytic activity of Au-based catalyst could be enhanced by adding inorganic bases [9]. Weak bases, such as Na2CO3 and K2CO3, are generally utilized in the catalytic systems, particularly in the reactions involving proton extraction [9]. Similarly, we found previously that in aqueous solution, the reaction rate of VA catalyzed by Au/GQDs under alkaline conditions were much higher than that of acidic conditions [3]. Hence, to further improve the catalytic activity of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4, bases were introduced. In the presence of K2CO3 (Fig. 3c), the oxidation rate of VA speeded up remarkably. The conversion of VA to veratraldehyde within 36h reaches to 35% at 100°C, which is much higher than that without base (Fig. 3a, 6% at 100°C). At 140°C, the conversion further improves to 92%. The conversion rate of VA to veratraldehyde increases from 41% to >99% within 36h at 100°C, while the ratio of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 to K2CO3 increases from 1:1 to 3:1 ( Table 1). Apparently, the ratio of the catalyst verse base is one of critical factors that affect the catalytic performance of the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4. This result is different from the reaction in aqueous solution with Au nanoparticles as catalyst, in which excess carbonate is required to achieve high catalytic efficiency [9]. Notably, the addition of K2CO3 does not influence the selectivity of VA oxidation to veratraldehyde (Fig. 3c). Moreover, Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites maintained 71% catalytic activity and >99% selectivity after four recycles (Fig. 3d), showing good reusability. The decrease of activity may be due to the enrichment of organic molecules covering the active site, and/or the silica layer was damaged by K2CO3.

To understand the functional role of each component of the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 in its catalytic performance, the oxidation of VA was carried out with Au/GQDs or Fe3O4 nanoparticles. After 36h of reaction at 140°C (Table S1), no oxidation of VA occurrs with Fe3O4 nanoparticles alone, and only 3% of VA converts to veratraldehyde with Au/GQDs, which is much lower than that (~22%) in the presence of Au/GQDs/Fe3O4. These results indicate that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles not only make the separation and recovery of the catalyst conveniently, but also enhance the catalytic activity of the Au/GQDs.

Au/GQDs showed excellent catalytic performance to the oxidation of aromatic alcohols, due to the good affinity between the aromatic basal planes of GQDs and aromatic alcohols, also the high reaction activity of aromatic alcohols. As depicted in Table 1, Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 exhibited the similar specificity to the aromatic substrates; the aromatic alcohols can be oxidized to corresponding aldehydes with high selectivity (>99%) and conversion.

In summary, Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 with unique hierarchal structure was fabricated. The as-prepared ternary composite exhibited high conversion and selectivity to the oxidation of VA and other aromatic alcohols under solvent-free condition. The catalytic activity of the ternary composite can be improved further by introducing small amount of K2CO3. The composite can be separated easily from the reaction system based on the superparamagnetic property of Fe3O4. Notably, the catalytic activity of the Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 is much higher than that of Au/GQDs or Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Given the high selectivity, conversion rate, and good reusability, we envisage that Au/GQDs/Fe3O4 composites should find practical application as catalysts in alcohol oxidations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was carried out with financial support from the national “973 Program” of China (Nos. 2014CB260411 and 2015CB931801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.07.105.
